description: Curated, qualitative essay-craft PATTERNS used to ground Narrative & Essay Studio feedback (RAG). These are NOT real student essays and contain NO student PII and NO numbers. Each row is a public writing-craft principle distilled from published admissions-writing guidance, tagged provenance 'curated_public' with a source_url. The feedback engine retrieves the closest patterns and references them; it never reproduces or drafts essay prose.

patterns.pattern: Strong essays show one concrete, sensory moment instead of summarizing a whole activity or trait. Look for a scene the reader can picture; flag paragraphs that only assert qualities ('I am passionate, hardworking') without a grounded moment that demonstrates them.

The essay should reveal how the student thinks and what an experience meant, not re-list accomplishments already in the activities section. Flag passages that restate awards or titles; prompt the student to add the 'so what' — what changed in how they see the work.

The most effective essays sound like the student speaking, with their own diction and rhythm, not a thesaurus-polished register. Flag sentences whose vocabulary or tone is sharply inconsistent with the rest of the essay; keep suggestions inside the student's existing voice.

Supplement essays must directly engage what the school asked (e.g. 'why this program', 'a community you belong to'). Flag supplements that are generic and could be submitted to any school; prompt the student to connect to specifics they actually care about.

A clear before/after or change-over-time gives the essay momentum. Flag essays that stay static (one mood, one realization stated up front); prompt for the turn where the student's understanding shifted.

Strong openings drop the reader into a specific moment rather than a broad abstraction ('Ever since I was young...'). Flag generic or cliche openers; suggest starting from a concrete detail already present later in the student's draft.

A strong 150-character activity entry leads with a vivid verb and names a concrete responsibility and its effect, trimming filler words and articles. Flag entries that only name a title or use vague verbs like 'helped' or 'participated'; suggest a stronger verb and the measurable-but-self-reported impact the student already mentioned.

The essays and activities should reinforce a coherent picture, not contradict each other or repeat the same anecdote. Flag when the same story carries multiple essays, or when a claimed central interest never appears elsewhere in the application.

Effective program supplements cite specific courses, labs, traditions, or opportunities and tie them to the student's stated goals. Flag praise that is interchangeable across schools ('great reputation', 'beautiful campus'); prompt the student toward specifics that match what the school states it values.

Early drafts often open with throat-clearing context before the real story begins. Flag long setup paragraphs that delay the first concrete moment; suggest cutting toward the line where the student's actual story starts.
